title: Alcaraz's French Open Threepeat Dream Crushed by Wrist Injury
summary:
Tennis fans, brace yourselves: Carlos Alcaraz, the two-time defending French Open champion, will not compete in this year's tournament. The 22-year-old Spaniard announced on Friday that tests on his right wrist revealed he needs to shut down his clay-court season to avoid long-term damage.

Alcaraz sustained the injury during the first round of the Barcelona Open last week, where he beat Otto Virtanen but subsequently pulled out. He withdrew from the Madrid Masters on April 17, raising concerns about his availability for the French Open. Now, those concerns are confirmed: he'll also miss the Italian Open in Rome.

"We have decided that the most prudent thing to do is to be cautious and not participate in Rome or Roland Garros," Alcaraz said on social media. "This is a difficult time for me, but I'm sure we will come out of it stronger."

This is a devastating blow for Alcaraz, who has dominated on clay. He won the French Open in 2024 and 2025, and in January he became the youngest man in history to complete the career Grand Slam (winning all four major tournaments) by triumphing at the Australian Open. He holds a 22-3 record this season and won a title in Doha. But after losing the Monte Carlo Masters final to Jannik Sinner on April 12, he dropped from world number one to number two.

Sinner, who has never won the French Open, expressed sympathy for his rival. "It's sad news for all of us, me being a competitor you want to play against the best players in the world, and he's definitely the best player on this surface," Sinner said after winning a match at the Madrid Open. "Being that young like he is and like I am, we need to look at our bodies first before worse things [happen]." Sinner hopes Alcaraz will be back for Wimbledon, the year's third Grand Slam, which starts in July.

Alcaraz admitted earlier this week that he's willing to sacrifice tournaments for his long-term health. "I'd rather come back maybe a bit later, but in great shape, than come back quickly and risk making this injury worse," he explained. "I have a long career ahead of me, so I'm not afraid to miss what I have to miss in order to recover as well as possible."

This will be just the second Grand Slam Alcaraz has missed since his main-draw debut at the 2021 Australian Open. The first was the 2023 Australian Open, which he missed with a hamstring injury. The French Open runs from May 24 to June 7, and without Alcaraz, the men's draw just got a lot more open.
why_it_matters: Alcaraz's injury and decision highlight the tension between short-term glory and long-term health in professional sports. For 12-14 year olds, this shows that even world-class athletes must prioritize their bodies over immediate success, a lesson that applies to any pursuit — from academics to hobbies. It also underscores how a single injury can change the outcome of a major tournament, affecting the competitive landscape.

keywords.explanation: Two-time defending French Open champion from Spain, ranked number 2, who will miss the tournament due to a right wrist injury.
Also called Roland Garros, a Grand Slam tennis tournament played on clay; Alcaraz won it in 2024 and 2025.
Winning all four major tournaments (Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, US Open); Alcaraz completed it in January 2025.
Italian player, Alcaraz's rival, ranked number 1 after Monte Carlo; expressed sadness and called Alcaraz the best on clay.
The tournament where Alcaraz first injured his wrist; he beat Otto Virtanen but then withdrew.

background_read: Carlos Alcaraz burst onto the tennis scene in 2022, winning the US Open at age 19 and becoming the youngest world number one in ATP history. He is known for his explosive power and finesse on all surfaces, but especially on clay.

The French Open (Roland Garros) is the second Grand Slam of the year and has been dominated by Rafael Nadal, who has won it 14 times. Nadal also missed the 2023 French Open due to injury, showing how even the greatest players are vulnerable to physical setbacks.

The career Grand Slam is a rare achievement for men's tennis; only eight men have done it, including Roger Federer, Novak Djokovic, and Rafael Nadal. Alcaraz completed it at age 22, tying with Bjorn Borg for the youngest to do so.

Tennis injuries are common because of the repetitive strain on joints. Wrist injuries in particular can be tricky for tennis players because they affect gripping and shot-making. Many players, including Juan Martin del Potro, have had careers derailed by wrist problems.
Article_Structure: LEAD: 'Tennis fans, brace yourselves: Carlos Alcaraz, the two-time defending French Open champion, will not compete in this year's tournament.'
└─ specific: 'The 22-year-old Spaniard announced on Friday that tests on his right wrist revealed he needs to shut down his clay-court season.'
KEY EVENT: Injury timeline and announcement.
├─ WHEN: Injury during Barcelona Open first round; withdrew from Madrid Masters on April 17; announcement on Friday.
├─ WHAT: Alcaraz will miss both Rome and Roland Garros.
└─ QUOTE: Alcaraz's social media statement about being cautious.
├─ Career Grand Slam: completed in January at Australian Open.
├─ Record: 22-3 season, Doha title, but lost Monte Carlo final to Sinner on April 12.
└─ Ranking drop: from world No.1 to No.2.
├─ Quote: sad news, wants to play best players, Alcaraz best on clay.
└─ Sinner's hope for Alcaraz's return at Wimbledon.
ALCARAZ'S ATTITUDE: Willingness to sacrifice tournaments for long-term health.
├─ Quote: 'I'd rather come back maybe a bit later, but in great shape.'
HISTORICAL NOTE: Only second Grand Slam missed since 2021 main-draw debut; first was 2023 Australian Open (hamstring).
CLOSING: French Open runs May 24-June 7; men's draw now more open without Alcaraz.

perspectives.description: Other players, especially Jannik Sinner, see the draw opening up. Sinner, who has never won the French Open, now has a better chance. Fans of upsets might enjoy seeing a new champion. Also, Alcaraz's cautious approach sets a good example for young athletes about not overtraining.

Fans are disappointed to miss the chance to watch Alcaraz chase a threepeat. Some may criticize the intense tennis schedule that leads to injuries. The tournament loses one of its biggest draws, which could affect viewership and excitement.

Alcaraz's injury is a reminder that tennis careers are fragile. He is young and has a long career ahead, but missing majors can affect his ranking and legacy. The coincidence of his previous injury also at the Australian Open suggests a pattern of vulnerability. The article frames this as a 'devastating blow' but also a prudent decision.

If Alcaraz recovers fully, his absence might fuel a comeback narrative for Wimbledon or the US Open. However, if the wrist becomes a recurring problem, it could limit his potential. The tennis world will watch closely how he manages this injury and whether young stars like Sinner or Holger Rune step up to fill the void.
